alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed space-frequency encoding network (SFE-Net) can directly estimate the aberrated point spread functions (PSFs) from biological images.

alignments.rationale: The publication preserves this technical result verbatim, indicating no change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The optical aberration can be computationally removed by a deconvolution algorithm using the estimated PSFs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Optical aberration can be computationally removed by the deconvolution algorithm using the estimated PSFs from SFE-Net.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result about computational removal of aberration using estimated PSFs, matching the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The combination of SFE-Net and the aberration-aware deep-learning super-resolution model (SFT-DFCAN) enables instant digital adaptive optics and optical aberration-aware super-resolution reconstruction for live-cell imaging.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The combination of SFE-Net and the aberration-aware deep-learning super-resolution model SFT-DFCAN enables instant digital adaptive optics and optical aberration-aware super-resolution reconstruction for live-cell imaging.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this combined-methods contribution exactly, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The adapted deep learning framework successfully detected Bornean white-bearded gibbon vocalisations with an F score of 0.87.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best model achieved an F score of 0.87, identifying 98% of the highest-quality calls from 90 hours of manually annotated audio recordings.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the identical F score and detection result; the wording is effectively the same result statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model identified 98% of the highest-quality calls from 90 hours of manually-annotated audio recordings.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best model achieved an F score of 0.87, identifying 98% of the highest-quality calls from 90 hours of manually annotated audio recordings.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same quantitative identification result (98% of high-quality calls) and the same dataset size.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There was no significant difference in the temporal distribution of great call detections between manual annotations and the model’s output.
alignments.pub_claim_text: No significant difference was found in the temporal distribution of great call detections between manual annotations and the model's output.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this null finding verbatim; meaning and scope are unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The application of the deep learning model can greatly reduce analysis times compared to human observers.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning model significantly reduced analysis times compared to a human observer.
alignments.rationale: The publication makes the claim more definitive (reports a significant reduction and higher certainty) compared with the preprint's tentative wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study provides a roadmap for applying deep learning to identify vocalisations of endangered species.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A roadmap for applying deep learning to identify vocalizations of species of interest was presented, which can be adapted for monitoring other endangered vocalizing species.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the roadmap and explicitly notes adaptability to other endangered vocalizing species, broadening the stated applicability.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepUMQA, a deep-learning method based on Ultrafast Shape Recognition (USR), significantly improves the accuracy of residue-level single-model quality assessment.
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepUMQA, a deep-learning method based on Ultrafast Shape Recognition (USR), significantly improves model assessment accuracy for residue-level single-model quality assessment.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same principal result about improved residue-level assessment accuracy using DeepUMQA.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The introduction of the residue-level USR feature allows for a better description of the topological relationship between residues and overall structure.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The introduction of the residue-level USR feature allows for a better description of the topological relationship between the residue and overall structure.
alignments.rationale: The publication elevates the certainty of this contribution (medium→high), clarifying and strengthening the claim about the USR feature's descriptive power.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Experimental results demonstrate that USR complements the voxelization feature to comprehensively characterize residue structure information.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Experimental results demonstrate that USR can supplement voxelization features to comprehensively characterize residue structure information.
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same empirical finding that USR complements/supplements voxelization for residue characterization.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepUMQA outperformed state-of-the-art single-model quality assessment methods, including ProQ2, ProQ3, ProQ3D, Ornate, VoroMQA, and DeepAccNet.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance of DeepUMQA ranks among the top in comparison to state-of-the-art single-model quality assessment methods.
alignments.rationale: The publication tones down the absolute 'outperformed' assertion to a more conservative 'ranks among the top', indicating a revision and reduced confidence in the superiority claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The U-Net model yielded lower mean absolute error (MAE) on training data compared to the standard feed-forward convolutional neural network (CNN).
alignments.pub_claim_text: The U-Net model yielded lower mean absolute error (MAE) on training data compared to the CNN model.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same training MAE comparison between U-Net and CNN models.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: On test data, the U-Net achieved a mean absolute error of 8.4±1.4 ml/100g/min for cerebral blood flow (CBF) and 0.22±0.09 s for arterial transit time (ATT).
alignments.pub_claim_text: On test data, the U-Net achieved an MAE of 8.4 ± 1.4 mL/100 g/min for cerebral blood flow (CBF) and 0.22 ± 0.09 s for arterial transit time (ATT).
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the identical quantitative test MAE results for CBF and ATT.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Model uncertainty, estimated with Monte Carlo dropout, was associated with model error.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A significant association was observed between mean absolute error and Monte Carlo dropout-based uncertainty estimates.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the association more explicitly as a significant observed relationship, clarifying the original association claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Hemodynamic parameter estimation from 1.5T and 3T multi-PLD ASL MRI is feasible and fast with a deep learning image-generation approach.
alignments.pub_claim_text: It is feasible to generate CBF and ATT maps from 1.5T and 3T multi-PLD ASL MRI using a fast deep learning image-generation approach.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the feasibility and speed conclusion for generating CBF and ATT maps with the deep learning approach.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: RaptorX yielded the best contact prediction among 46 groups in the CASP13 FM targets.
alignments.pub_claim_text: RaptorX yielded the best contact prediction among 46 groups in the CASP13 FM targets.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this benchmark result verbatim; there is no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: RaptorX achieved top L/5, L/2 and L long-range contact precision of 70%, 58% and 45%, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: RaptorX achieved top L/5, L/2, and L long-range contact precision of 70%, 58%, and 45%, respectively.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the identical long-range contact precision metrics.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: RaptorX predicted correct folds (TMscore>0.5) for 18 of 32 targets.
alignments.pub_claim_text: RaptorX predicted correct folds (TMscore > 0.5) for 18 of 32 targets in CASP13.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same folding success result for the CASP13 targets.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Predicted distance is more useful than contacts for both template-based and free modeling.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Predicted distance is more useful than contacts for both template-based and free modeling.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same methodological conclusion about the utility of predicted distances.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Structure modeling may be improved by integrating alignment and co-evolutionary information via deep learning.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Structure modeling may be improved by integrating template and coevolutionary information via deep learning.
alignments.rationale: The publication substitutes 'template' for 'alignment', a modest revision in the type of information proposed for integration; the general suggestion remains.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our deep convolutional neural network (CNN) for inferring quartet topologies from multiple sequence alignments is highly accurate and often outperforms traditional methods.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our deep convolutional neural network (CNN) can infer quartet topologies from multiple sequence alignments with high accuracy on simulated data.
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies the evaluation context ('on simulated data'), narrowing the original broader phrasing while preserving the high-accuracy claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The CNN is remarkably robust to bias-inducing regions of parameter space such as the Felsenstein zone and the Farris zone.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly report robustness to Felsenstein/Farris zone biases; this specific robustness claim is absent in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The confidence scores produced by our CNN can more accurately assess support for the chosen topology than bootstrap and posterior probability scores from traditional methods.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The confidence scores produced by our CNN can more accurately assess support for the chosen topology than bootstrap and posterior probability scores from traditional methods.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this comparative claim about confidence-score accuracy without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning approaches such as ours have the potential to produce more accurate phylogenetic inferences.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning approaches such as ours have the potential to produce more accurate phylogenetic inferences.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same overall conclusion but lowers the expressed certainty (publication labels it as low certainty), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Rest2vec uses the phase angle spatial embedding method with dimensionality reduction techniques to embed the functional connectome into lower dimensions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Rest2vec uses the phase angle spatial embedding (PhASE) method with dimensionality reduction to embed the connectome into lower dimensions.
alignments.rationale: Q0 is essentially the same claim as P0 with minor naming (PhASE) preserved; meaning and confidence are unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Rest2vec can be linked to the maximum mean discrepancy metric to assign functional modules of the connectome in a continuous manner.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The 'functional distance' between brain regions in the rest2vec space can be applied to discover biologically-relevant connectivity gradients.
alignments.rationale: The preprint emphasized a connection to MMD and continuous module assignment; the paper reframes this as a functional-distance application to gradients, shifting the claim focus and dropping the explicit MMD framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The use of rest2vec improves upon traditional binary classification methods in studying the functional connectome.
alignments.rationale: No publication claim asserts an improvement specifically over binary classification methods; this conclusion from the preprint is not represented in the paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Resting-state functional magnetic resonance imaging connectomics has inherent analytical challenges, such as accounting for negative correlations.
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed limitation (functional vs anatomical distance) does not restate the preprint's point about negative correlations, so P3 appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: fastISM speeds up in-silico saturation mutagenesis (ISM) by a factor of over 10x for commonly used convolutional neural network architectures.
alignments.pub_claim_text: fastISM is an algorithm that speeds up in silico saturation mutagenesis by a factor of over 10× for commonly used convolutional neural network architectures.
alignments.rationale: Q0 restates P0 verbatim as a core performance result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The majority of computation in ISM is spent in convolutional layers, and a single mutation only disrupts a limited region of intermediate layers, rendering most computation redundant.
alignments.rationale: The publication's claims summarize algorithmic speedups but do not explicitly include this mechanistic explanation about convolutional-layer locality.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: fastISM reduces the gap between backpropagation-based feature attribution methods and ISM.
alignments.pub_claim_text: fastISM reduces the gap between backpropagation-based feature attribution methods and in silico saturation mutagenesis.
alignments.rationale: Q1 conveys the same comparative claim about narrowing the gap between ISM and backprop-based attribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: fastISM far surpasses the runtime of backpropagation-based methods on multi-output architectures, making it feasible to run ISM on a large number of sequences.
alignments.pub_claim_text: fastISM far surpasses the runtime of backpropagation-based methods on multi-output architectures.
alignments.rationale: Q2 matches the runtime-surpass aspect of P3 directly; the publication separates the feasibility conclusion into a distinct claim (Q3).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed ConvNet architectures predict contacts with significantly more precision than the architectures used in several state-of-the-art methods.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed ConvNet architectures predict contacts with significantly more precision than the architectures used in several state-of-the-art methods.
alignments.rationale: Q0 is a direct restatement of P0.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: When trained using the DeepCov dataset, our architectures achieve up to 15% higher precision when L/2 long-range contacts are evaluated.
alignments.pub_claim_text: When trained using the DeepCov dataset, our architectures achieve up to 15% higher precision when L/2 long-range contacts are evaluated.
alignments.rationale: Q1 repeats P1 exactly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our single network achieves 4.8% higher precision than the ensembled DNCON2 method when top L long-range contacts are evaluated.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our single network achieves 4.8% higher precision than the ensembled DNCON2 method when top L long-range contacts are evaluated.
alignments.rationale: Q2 matches P2 verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Models specifically trained on 3’UTR sequences demonstrate superior performance when compared to the established genome-wide foundation models in three out of four downstream tasks.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Models specifically trained on 3'UTR sequences demonstrate superior performance when compared to established genome-wide foundation models in three out of four downstream tasks.
alignments.rationale: Q0 restates P0 with equivalent meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study investigates the efficacy of various unsupervised approaches, including genome-wide and 3’UTR-specific foundation models on human 3’UTR regions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study investigates the efficacy of various unsupervised approaches, including genome-wide and 3' untranslated region (3'UTR)-specific foundation models on human 3'UTR regions.
alignments.rationale: Q1 mirrors P1's contribution description.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our results underscore the importance of considering genome partitioning into functional regions when training and evaluating foundation models.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our results underscore the importance of considering genome partitioning into distinct functional regions when training and evaluating foundation models.
alignments.rationale: Q2 conveys the same conclusion as P2 with equivalent scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed set of 3’UTR-specific tasks can be used for benchmarking future models.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed set of 3'UTR-specific tasks can be used for benchmarking of future models.
alignments.rationale: Q3 repeats P3's contribution claim directly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SynSurr estimates the same genetic effect as standard genome-wide association studies (GWAS) of the target phenotype.
alignments.pub_claim_text: SynSurr estimates the same genetic effect as standard GWAS and improves power in proportion to the quality of the imputations.
alignments.rationale: Q1 includes the same point about estimating the same genetic effect and adds information about power; thus P0 is preserved but clarified with additional quantification.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SynSurr improves power provided the synthetic surrogate is correlated with the target phenotype.
alignments.pub_claim_text: SynSurr estimates the same genetic effect as standard GWAS and improves power in proportion to the quality of the imputations.
alignments.rationale: P1's conditional power-improvement claim is reflected in Q1, which frames improvement proportional to imputation quality (a clearer formulation of the same idea).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Unlike imputation or proxy analysis, SynSurr does not require that the synthetic surrogate is obtained from a correctly specified generative model.
alignments.pub_claim_text: SynSurr requires a commonly made missing-at-random assumption but relaxes the requirements of existing imputation methods by not requiring correct model specification.
alignments.rationale: Q2 restates P2 while adding the MAR assumption caveat, so the original point is preserved but clarified with an explicit assumed condition.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Imputing missing phenotypes can invalidate statistical inference when the imputation model is misspecified.
alignments.pub_claim_text: SynSurr requires a commonly made missing-at-random assumption but relaxes the requirements of existing imputation methods by not requiring correct model specification.
alignments.rationale: P3's limitation about misspecified imputations is addressed by Q2's claim that SynSurr relaxes model-specification requirements; the paper reframes the limitation in the context of assumptions and method robustness.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Intra-tumor signals exist and are correlated with alterations in healthy tissue and resting-state networks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We find intertwined alterations in the frequency domain of local and spatially distributed resting-state functional signals, potentially arising within the tumor. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication rephrases and adds a frequency-domain detail but conveys the same finding that tumor-associated local and distributed resting-state signals are altered; this is a clarification rather than a substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A fiber tracking pipeline has been proposed that uses anatomical information to reconstruct bundles in tumoral and peritumoral tissue. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We propose a fiber tracking pipeline capable of using anatomical information while still reconstructing bundles in tumoral and peritumoral tissue. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same contribution with essentially identical scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning can predict structural rearrangement after surgery based on the preoperative brain network. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using machine learning and healthy anatomical information, we predict structural rearrangement after surgery given the preoperative brain network. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds the detail that healthy anatomical information was used as input, clarifying the preprint claim without changing its scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The generative model disentangles complex patterns of connectivity reorganization for different types of tumors. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The generative model disentangles complex patterns of connectivity reorganization for different types of tumors. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim is carried forward verbatim with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Careful study design is important as MR signals within damaged brain tissues exhibit non-trivial patterns of structural and functional (dis-)connections or activity. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We show the importance of carefully designing studies including MR signals within damaged brain tissues, as they exhibit and relate to non-trivial patterns of both structural and functional (dis-)connections or activity. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same conclusion and emphasis on study design and MR signal complexity without changing scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: scIDPMs utilizes conditional diffusion probabilistic models to impute scRNA-seq data effectively. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The novel method scIDPMs utilizes conditional diffusion probabilistic models to impute scRNA-seq data. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the methodological contribution directly and without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of scIDPMs was evaluated using both simulated and real scRNA-seq datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's summarized claims do not mention the specific evaluation on simulated and real datasets, so this preprint-level claim is not reflected in the listed publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: scIDPMs exhibited exceptional performance in recovering biologically meaningful gene expression values compared to eight other imputation methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: scIDPMs outperforms other imputation methods in restoring biologically meaningful gene expression values. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same performance conclusion but in slightly more general terms (drops the explicit "eight" comparator), so the claim is clarified rather than changed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Existing imputation methods struggle to fully capture the distribution of dropout values due to the high sparsity of scRNA-seq data. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Existing imputation methods struggle to capture dropout value distributions due to the sparsity of scRNA-seq data. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The limitation is restated in the publication with equivalent meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepAIR significantly improves the prediction accuracy of the binding reactivity between adaptive immune receptors and antigens by integrating predicted AIR structures using AlphaFold2. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepAIR improves the AIR-antigen binding prediction that facilitates the study of adaptive immunity. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the improvement claim and supplies concrete performance metrics elsewhere in the claims, which strengthens (amplifies) confidence while keeping the original claim's scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepAIR features a novel deep learning architecture that effectively extracts and integrates informative features from three different feature encoders. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication's headline claims emphasize results and broad impact but do not state the specific architectural contribution in the listed publication claims, so no direct match is present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepAIR is implemented as a biologically interpretable deep learning framework that highlights key residues in both α and β chains critical for predicting AIR-antigen binding. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Interpretability and residue-level attribution are not represented in the publication claim set provided, so this preprint claim has no corresponding publication claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed deep learning-based image analysis pipeline achieves a high fidelity result with a 1% error rate without human supervision. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed deep learning-based image analysis pipeline achieves a 1% error rate without human intervention. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the performance claim verbatim (1% error) and retains the same confidence and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm processes a typical frame containing approximately 150 cells in less than 700 milliseconds. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The algorithm processes a typical frame containing approximately 150 cells in less than 700 milliseconds. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This operational performance claim is carried over unchanged into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The framework is not constrained to a particular experimental setup and has the potential to generalize to time-lapse images of other organisms or different experimental configurations. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The framework is capable of generalizing to time-lapse images of other organisms or different experimental configurations. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the generalizability claim with equivalent wording and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The advances in this image analysis pipeline open the door to applications such as real-time tracking of gene expression and high throughput analysis of strain libraries at single-cell resolution. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This innovative approach combines cell segmentation, tracking, and lineage reconstruction using machine learning. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the contribution in terms of the integrated capabilities (segmentation, tracking, lineage), which clarifies how the pipeline enables the applications mentioned in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Brain decoding with graph neural networks achieves state-of-the-art performance on the human connectome project benchmark. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims emphasize model design, accuracy, robustness and principles but do not explicitly state a SOTA result on the HCP benchmark, so this specific benchmark claim is not reflected.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The optimal choice for large-scale cognitive decoding is to propagate neural dynamics within empirical functional connectomes and integrate brain dynamics using high-order graph convolutions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The optimal choice for large-scale cognitive decoding is to propagate neural dynamics within empirical functional connectomes and integrate brain dynamics using high-order graph convolutions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This methodological recommendation is carried forward verbatim into the publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The trained model exhibits high decoding accuracy and robustness against adversarial attacks on the graph architecture. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model exhibits high decoding accuracy and robustness against adversarial attacks on the graph architecture. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the performance and robustness claim directly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study establishes principles of how to effectively leverage human connectome constraints in deep graph neural networks. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study establishes principles of how to effectively leverage human connectome constraints in deep graph neural networks. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this high-level contribution/principle with equivalent meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Both fine-tuned models demonstrated nearly perfect performance, averaging over 99% accuracy when phenotype terms for normalization were included in the fine-tuning corpora. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Both fine-tuned models demonstrated nearly perfect performance, averaging over 99% accuracy when phenotype terms for normalization were included in the fine-tuning corpora. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint and publication statements are effectively identical in wording and reported result, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ChatGPT-3.5 has only ~20% accuracy in identifying HPO IDs for phenotype terms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ChatGPT-3.5 has only ∼20% accuracy in identifying HPO IDs for phenotype terms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same numerical performance claim for ChatGPT-3.5 with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The accuracy of the NAME and NAME+SYN models is 10.2% and 36.1%, respectively, when single-character typos are introduced in the phenotype terms, but increases to 61.8% with additional typo-specific fine-tuning. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the typo-robustness results or the specific typo-specific fine-tuning improvement, so this preprint claim was omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The NAME model achieved 11.2% accuracy for unseen synonyms sourced from HPO vocabularies, while the NAME+SYN model achieved 92.7% accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The NAME+SYN model achieved 92.7% accuracy for terms sourced from HPO vocabularies as unseen synonyms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the high-performing NAME+SYN result but omits the low NAME baseline (11.2%), narrowing the reported comparison and thus revising the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our fine-tuned models demonstrate the ability to normalize phenotype terms unseen in the fine-tuning corpus, including misspellings, synonyms, terms from other ontologies, and laymen's terms. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our fine-tuned models demonstrate the ability to normalize phenotype terms unseen in the fine-tuning corpus, including misspellings and synonyms. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same general conclusion but lists fewer categories (misspellings and synonyms only), narrowing the original broader claim about other ontologies and lay terms.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Transformers have not yet been applied to brain age prediction. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Transformers may outperform CNNs in predicting brain age. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint claimed transformers were not applied, whereas the publication asserts transformers may outperform CNNs — a substantive change in stance and scope, with reduced certainty (high→medium).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SwinT and ResNet performed on par in predicting brain age. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SwinT and ResNet performed on par in predicting brain age, with SwinT likely to surpass ResNet in prediction accuracy with additional training data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the parity result but adds nuance that SwinT may surpass ResNet with more data, clarifying and slightly amplifying the original result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Different model architectures may characterize different (sub-)sets of brain aging effects. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Different deep learning model architectures potentially capture different (sub-)sets of brain aging effects. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same conclusion with similar wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We did not find substantial differences in the structure of brain age predictions between model architectures. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There are no substantial differences in the structure of brain age predictions across the model architectures. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is reported equivalently in the publication with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The choice of deep learning model architecture does not appear to have a confounding effect on brain age studies. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The choice of deep learning model architecture does not appear to have a confounding effect on brain age studies. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains this conclusion verbatim, with no substantive change in meaning or certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study presents an innovative XAI framework to identify and validate key genetic biomarkers for HCC prognosis. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study identifies and validates key genetic biomarkers for HCC prognosis using explainable artificial intelligence. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the contribution/result using equivalent language about XAI identifying and validating HCC biomarkers.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The identified biomarkers such as TOP3B, SSBP3, and COX7A2L improve the predictive accuracy for HCC prognosis beyond AFP. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Biomarkers such as TOP3B, SSBP3, and COX7A2L improve the predictive accuracy for HCC prognosis beyond Alpha-fetoprotein. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The same biomarker claim appears in the publication but with reduced stated certainty (high→medium), so the content is unchanged but confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The application of XAI in biomarker discovery represents a significant advancement in HCC research. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The application of XAI in biomarker discovery represents a significant advancement in HCC research. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same conclusion but expresses it with higher certainty (medium→high), indicating amplified confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study emphasizes the relevance of these biomarkers to the Hispanic population. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study emphasizes the relevance of identified biomarkers to the Hispanic population. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Both versions highlight the biomarkers' relevance to the Hispanic population with similar wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepSPT is a deep learning framework that interprets the diffusional 2D or 3D temporal behavior of objects rapidly and efficiently. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepSPT is a deep learning framework that automates the analysis of diffusional behavior of objects in living systems. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes automation and application to living systems; this clarifies and slightly rephrases the preprint's description without changing the core contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepSPT can automate the mapping of early events of viral infections with up to 95% accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The application of DeepSPT allows for the mapping of early events of viral infections within seconds instead of weeks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes rapid mapping speed rather than the single-number accuracy claim; while related, the focus and metrics differ, so the claim is revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepSPT extracts biological information from diffusion alone, indicating that motion encodes function at the molecular and subcellular level. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepSPT effectively extracts biological information from diffusion, indicating that motion encodes function at the molecular and subcellular level. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this conceptual conclusion with essentially identical phrasing and certainty.

alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepSPT can identify endosomal organelles, clathrin-coated pits, and vesicles with F1 scores of 81%, 82%, and 95%, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports explicit organelle-identification performance metrics that were not present in the preprint, so this is an added specific result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A novel computational approach called NuFold has been proposed to accurately predict RNA tertiary structures. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: NuFold is a novel computational approach that leverages state-of-the-art deep learning architecture to accurately predict RNA tertiary structures. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication refines the description by specifying the use of state-of-the-art deep learning, clarifying but not changing the core contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NuFold aims to provide a cost-effective and efficient means of bridging the gap between RNA sequence information and structural comprehension. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not make the explicit claim about cost-effectiveness or this particular framing of aims, so the preprint's wording about cost/efficiency was omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NuFold implements a nucleobase center representation that allows it to reproduce all possible nucleotide conformations accurately. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: NuFold exhibited a particular advantage in building correct local geometries of RNA. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the technical result as an advantage in local geometry reconstruction, which clarifies the preprint's statement about reproducing nucleotide conformations.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Benchmark study showed that NuFold clearly outperformed energy-based methods and demonstrated comparable results with existing state-of-the-art deep-learning-based methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds benchmarking results versus other methods that were not present in the preprint text provided, so this is an added claim.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Analyses of individual components in the NuFold pipeline indicated that the performance improved by utilizing metagenome sequences for multiple sequence alignment and increasing the number of recycling. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Component-level analyses and specific methodological improvements are reported in the publication but were not in the preprint, making this an added claim.

alignments.pub_claim_text: NuFold is also capable of predicting multimer complex structures of RNA by linking the input sequences. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports multimer-prediction capability not present in the preprint excerpt, so this is an added publication claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our cell-state-oriented framework significantly enhances the accuracy of genome-informed prediction of drug responses in comparison to models that directly use SGAs as inputs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our cell-state-oriented framework notably improves the accuracy of predictions of drug responses compared to models using SGAs directly in cell lines.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same improvement but specifies it applies in cell lines, narrowing the scope relative to the preprint while preserving the core result and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our framework enables the prediction of response to chemotherapy agents based on SGAs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our framework enables the prediction of responses to chemotherapy agents based on SGAs, thus expanding genome-informed precision oncology beyond molecularly targeted drugs.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the same core claim but expands its implication (extension beyond targeted drugs), clarifying and broadening the claimed impact.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed computational framework consists of a representation-learning component and a drug-response-prediction component.
alignments.rationale: The specific architectural decomposition described in the preprint is not represented among the publication claims and thus appears omitted from the published claim set.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Our model performs well with real patient data.
alignments.rationale: This claim about performance on patient data appears in the publication but was not present in the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pharmacological blockade of the κ-opioid receptor (KOR) abolishes brain reward deficits in rodents during withdrawal.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Pharmacological blockade of the κ-opioid receptor (KOR) abolishes brain reward deficits in rodents during withdrawal.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result verbatim; no change in meaning, scope, or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: KOR antagonists reduce the escalation of opioid use in rats with extended access to opioids.
alignments.pub_claim_text: KOR antagonists reduce the escalation of opioid use in rats with extended access to opioids.
alignments.rationale: The claim is preserved exactly between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Very few potent selective KOR antagonists are known to date, and most exhibit significant safety concerns.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Very few potent selective KOR antagonists are known to date, and most exhibit significant safety concerns.
alignments.rationale: Limitation statement is unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A generative deep learning framework was used for the de novo design of chemotypes with putative KOR antagonistic activity.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A generative deep-learning framework was used for the de novo design of chemotypes with putative KOR antagonistic activity.
alignments.rationale: The contribution claim is retained with equivalent meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The models and proposed training protocol were experimentally validated by binding and functional assays.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The models and proposed training protocol were experimentally validated by binding and functional assays.
alignments.rationale: Experimental validation claim is unchanged between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: RiboStrike is a deep learning framework that identifies small molecules against specific microRNAs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: RiboStrike is a deep-learning framework that identifies small molecules against specific microRNAs.
alignments.rationale: The contribution claim is restated in the publication with equivalent meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The application of RiboStrike to microRNA-21 resulted in the identification of eight small molecules with anti-miR-21 activity.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Eight small molecules were identified that showed anti-miR-21 activity in both reporter assays and RNA sequencing experiments.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides additional experimental detail (assays used) supporting the same result, clarifying how activity was demonstrated.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: One of the identified small molecules significantly reduced lung metastases in mouse models of breast cancer.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The top candidate compound demonstrated a significant reduction in lung metastases in a xenograft mouse model of breast cancer.
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies it was the top candidate and a xenograft model, narrowing the animal-model description while preserving the result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: RiboStrike effectively screens for microRNA-targeting compounds in cancer.
alignments.pub_claim_text: RiboStrike's ability to nominate compounds that target the activity of miRNAs in cancer is demonstrated through the results.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is maintained and supported by the reported results with equivalent scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Approximately 80% of visually responsive single neurons in human ventral temporal cortex use a distributed axis code to represent objects.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Approximately 80% of visually responsive single neurons in the human ventral temporal cortex (VTC) use a distributed axis code to represent objects.
alignments.rationale: The core quantitative result and interpretation are preserved exactly in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: About 40% of axis-tuned VTC neurons recapitulated the visual code when subjects imagined specific objects.
alignments.pub_claim_text: About 40% of axis-tuned VTC neurons recapitulated the visual code while subjects imagined specific objects.
alignments.rationale: The claim's content is the same but the publication reports reduced certainty for this result (preprint high → publication medium), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Visual imagery is supported by reactivation of the same neurons involved in perception.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Visual imagery is supported by reactivation of the same neurons involved in perception.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is carried over without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings provide single neuron evidence for the existence of a generative model in human VTC.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings provide single-neuron evidence for the existence of a generative model in human VTC.
alignments.rationale: The interpretive conclusion remains the same in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Data leakage significantly inflates prediction performance in neuroimaging predictive models when it occurs via feature selection and repeated subjects.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Leakage via feature selection and repeated subjects drastically inflates prediction performance.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this primary result (different wording but same meaning and emphasis).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Other forms of leakage, such as leaky site correction, have only minor effects on prediction performance, while leaky covariate regression can even decrease it.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Other forms of leakage have minor effects on prediction performance.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the general point that other leakages have minor effects but omits the specific preprint detail about leaky covariate regression decreasing performance, representing a revision.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Leakage not only affects prediction performance but also model coefficients, impacting neurobiological interpretations.
alignments.rationale: The specific claim about effects on model coefficients and neurobiological interpretation is not reflected among the publication claims and appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Predictive models using small datasets are more sensitive to the effects of data leakage.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Small datasets exacerbate the effects of leakage.
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates this result with stronger certainty (preprint medium → publication high), so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Understanding the effects of leakage on neuroimaging predictive models is crucial for improving the validity and reproducibility of predictive modeling.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Avoiding data leakage is important to improve the validity and reproducibility of predictive modeling.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the practical implication toward avoidance to achieve validity and reproducibility; the intended goal is the same but wording shifts emphasis, a clarification.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Understanding the effects of leakage can inform how it affects existing literature in neuroimaging.
alignments.rationale: This specific claim about informing interpretation of existing literature appears in the publication but was not present among the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AutoQTL captures the phenotypic variance explained under a standard additive model while also providing evidence of non-additive effects including deviations from additivity and 2-way epistatic interactions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: AutoQTL captures the phenotypic variance explained under a standard additive model.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the additive-variance claim verbatim but splits out non-additive findings into a separate claim, so the preprint's combined statement is clarified and narrowed to the additive component here.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Feature importance metrics provide different insights into the inheritance models and predictive power of multiple GWAS-derived putative QTL.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Feature importance metrics provide different insights into the inheritance models and predictive power of multiple GWAS-derived putative QTL.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result essentially verbatim with the same scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Automated machine learning techniques can be applied to genetic data and have the potential to detect both additive and non-additive effects via various optimal solutions and feature importance metrics.
alignments.pub_claim_text: AutoQTL detects evidence of non-additive effects including deviations from additivity and 2-way epistatic interactions in simulated data via multiple optimal solutions.
alignments.rationale: The publication narrows the preprint's broader methodological claim to specific evidence for non-additive effects in simulated data and specifies use of multiple optimal solutions, clarifying and narrowing the original statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proof-of-concept for AutoQTL illustrates the ability to automate complex decisions related to the analysis of complex traits.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Automated machine learning techniques can complement standard approaches and have the potential to detect both additive and non-additive effects.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the contribution more modestly (as complementing standard approaches and potential detection) rather than asserting an ability to automate complex decisions, so the claim is revised, narrowed, and presented with lower confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed computational pipeline using attention-based multi-instance learning models (ABMILMs) provides accurate diagnoses of acute leukemia with an AUROC of 0.961.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed computational pipeline using attention-based multi-instance learning models (ABMILMs) provides accurate diagnoses of acute leukemia with an AUROC of 0.961.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this quantitative diagnostic performance claim unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The pipeline accurately differentiates acute myeloid leukemia (AML) from B- and T-lymphoblastic leukemia with an AUROC of 0.965.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The pipeline accurately differentiates acute myeloid leukemia (AML) from B- and T-lymphoblastic leukemia with an AUROC of 0.965.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this performance claim identically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Models for predicting 9 cytogenetic aberrancies and 32 pathogenic variants in AML provide accurate predictions, particularly for specific variants such as t(15;17)(PML::RARA) with an AUROC of 0.929.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Models for predicting cytogenetic aberrancies and pathogenic variants in AML provide accurate predictions, particularly for specific variants such as t(15;17)(PML::RARA) with an AUROC of 0.929.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the predictive performance claim, with only minimal wording changes that do not alter meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study demonstrates the feasibility of using deep learning-based analysis of flow cytometric data for automated AML diagnosis and molecular characterization.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study demonstrates the feasibility of using deep learning-based analysis of flow cytometric data for automated AML diagnosis and molecular characterization.
alignments.rationale: The overarching feasibility conclusion is carried forward unchanged into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Recurrent neural network models trained to track position while reporting context changes produce activity patterns similar to population-wide remapping in the entorhinal cortex.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Recurrent neural network models trained to track position while reporting context changes produce activity patterns similar to population-wide remapping in the entorhinal cortex.
alignments.rationale: The key result about RNN activity matching entorhinal remapping is retained verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The combined task constraints of navigation and context inference lead to qualitatively similar neural activity patterns as observed in biological systems.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The combined task constraints of navigation and context inference lead to qualitatively similar neural activity patterns as observed in biological systems.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves this conclusion about task constraints producing biologically similar activity patterns.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The models developed in this study provide a simple and general framework for understanding remapping in neural circuits involved in navigation and context inference.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This research provides a simple, general, and experimentally-grounded model of remapping in neural circuits.
alignments.rationale: The publication strengthens and grounds the preprint's framing by emphasizing experimental grounding and raises certainty, so the claim is clarified and its confidence is amplified.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The models developed in this study identify a solution that generalizes to more complex navigation and inference tasks.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds an explicit claim about generalization to more complex tasks that was not present in the preprint text.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SKiM demonstrates the ability to discover useful A-B-C linkages in various control experiments, including classic LBD discoveries, drug repurposing, and cancer associations.
alignments.pub_claim_text: SKiM demonstrates the ability to discover useful A–B–C linkages in various control experiments, including classic LBD discoveries, drug repurposing, and cancer associations.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this demonstration of SKiM's discovery ability with essentially identical wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SKiM provides a simple and intuitive open-source web interface that allows users to perform searches with comprehensive lists of drugs, diseases, phenotypes, and symptoms.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The web interface of SKiM is designed to be simple and intuitive, enabling users to easily perform searches related to drugs, diseases, phenotypes, and symptoms.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides the same description of the web interface's design and functionality, preserving scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SKiM is generalized for any biomedical domain and can perform searches with many thousands of C term concepts.
alignments.pub_claim_text: SKiM is an open-source tool that improves upon existing literature-based discovery tools by allowing users to provide their own lists of B or C terms and query thousands of C terms.
alignments.rationale: The publication clarifies implementation details (open-source, user-provided lists) while preserving the claim of broad domain and large-scale C-term querying.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SKiM moves beyond simple identification of relationships by providing relationship type labels from a knowledge graph.
alignments.pub_claim_text: SKiM moves beyond simple relationship identification by providing relationship type labels from a knowledge graph built with transformer machine-learning models.
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies that the knowledge graph is built with transformer models and presents this conclusion with higher certainty, clarifying and slightly amplifying the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This work demonstrates the first example of goal-directed learning in brain organoids.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We demonstrate goal-directed learning in brain organoids through feedback-driven neural plasticity.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the demonstration of goal-directed learning but omits the stronger 'first example' language, so the claim is revised (weakened) though the core result remains.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Longitudinal experiments enabled by the closed-loop electrophysiology framework illuminate how different methods of selecting training signals enable improvement on the tasks.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the preprint's specific claim about longitudinal experiments enabled by the closed-loop framework, so this element appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Training signals chosen by artificial reinforcement learning yield better performance on the task than randomly chosen training signals or the absence of a training signal.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Training signals chosen by artificial reinforcement learning yield better performance than randomly chosen training signals or no training signal.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains this experimental result verbatim about reinforcement-learning-chosen signals improving performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This systematic approach to studying learning mechanisms in vitro opens new possibilities for therapeutic interventions and biological computation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This systematic approach to studying goal-directed neural plasticity mechanisms in vitro opens new possibilities for neural rehabilitation and biological computation.
alignments.rationale: The publication refines the potential applications language from broad therapeutic interventions to the more specific 'neural rehabilitation', clarifying the intended translational direction.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Optical measurement of dopamine and acetylcholine release in the dorsomedial striatum revealed distinct dynamics at task events. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Reward cues evoked cholinergic pauses with different phase relationships relative to dopamine. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies the stimulus (reward cues) and the cholinergic pauses/phase relationships, narrowing and clarifying the preprint's broader statement about distinct dynamics at task events.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: When dopamine slightly lagged cholinergic dips, dopamine predicted future behaviour and DMS firing rates on subsequent trials. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: When dopamine lagged cholinergic dips, dopamine predicted future behavior and DMS firing rates on subsequent trials. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this result with equivalent wording and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: When dopamine was coincident with cholinergic bursts, it preceded and predicted the vigour of upcoming contralateral orienting movements. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: When dopamine was coincident with cholinergic bursts, it preceded and predicted the vigor of contralateral orienting movements. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim is carried forward into the publication with equivalent content and emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The precise phase relationship between dopamine and acetylcholine allows dopamine to be used for either movement or learning depending on instantaneous behavioural context. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Cholinergic dynamics determine whether dopamine promotes vigor or learning, depending on the instantaneous behavioral context. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication rephrases the conclusion to emphasize cholinergic dynamics as the determining factor, clarifying the original idea while preserving the same scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Federated learning enables collaboration in medicine without the need to aggregate data in a central cloud. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Federated learning enables collaboration in medicine without the need to aggregate data in a central cloud. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Identical conclusion carried from preprint to publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Graph neural networks (GNNs) are particularly developed for graphs, which are common in the biomedical domain. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Graph neural networks (GNNs) are particularly developed for graphs, which are common in the biomedical domain. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Claim is the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Ensemble-GNN software package can be used to deploy federated, ensemble-based GNNs in Python. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Ensemble-GNN software package can be used to deploy federated, ensemble-based GNNs in Python. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Software contribution is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Ensemble-GNN allows for the quick building of predictive models utilizing PPI networks with various node features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Ensemble-GNN allows for the quick building of predictive models utilizing PPI networks with various node features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Result statement is reproduced unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Results from a public dataset of 981 patients and 8469 genes from the Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) are presented as an example. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Results from a public dataset of 981 patients and 8469 genes from the Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) are presented as an example. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Example dataset and results are reported identically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Extensive intratumor heterogeneity and epigenetic plasticity related to cell-state transitions are major roadblocks to successful drug therapy in GBM. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims do not include this high-level limitation about heterogeneity and plasticity, so the claim does not appear in the final set of publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The transcription factor TFAP2A can be seen to promote a transition from the NPC-like to the MES-like state. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The transcription factor TFAP2A promotes a transition from the NPC-like to the MES-like state in GBM. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the TFAP2A-driven transition with similar wording and level of confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A protein-protein interaction network (PPIN) was constructed for each cell state using phosphoproteomic measurements from GBM patients. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A protein–protein interaction network was constructed for each cell state, which was then pooled to form a single Boolean network for in silico protein knockout simulations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the PPIN construction claim but adds pooling into a Boolean network and subsequent simulations, expanding the scope and clarifying downstream analysis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The simulation results and machine learning predictions generated hypotheses for clinically relevant causal mechanisms of cell state transitions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The generated hypotheses suggest potential drug targets that can be further tested in vitro to support cell state-directed therapy. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the hypotheses more specifically as potential drug targets for in vitro testing, narrowing the original broader phrasing about causal mechanisms.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The combination of deep learning and physics-based methods allows for the generation of proteins with diverse and designable pocket geometries. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The generated family of proteins has diverse and designable pocket geometries for binding small molecules. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates that the designed proteins have diverse, designable pocket geometries; the contribution is carried through with similar meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The designed binders exhibit nanomolar to low micromolar binding affinities and atomic-level design accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Biophysical characterization of the designed binders reveals nanomolar to low micromolar binding affinities and atomic-level design accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Biophysical results are presented with equivalent specificity and confidence in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The design approach enables the creation of a biosensor with nanomolar sensitivity for cortisol. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We use a cortisol binder to design a chemically induced dimerization (CID) system that enables the construction of a biosensor for cortisol detection. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides more mechanistic detail (CID system) about how the cortisol biosensor was constructed, clarifying the original claim while preserving the result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This method provides a general approach to design proteins that can bind and sense small molecules for various applications. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The approach described here demonstrates the potential of the NTF2 fold and deep learning-based protein design in sensor development. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication narrows the general claim to sensor development using the NTF2 fold and frames it more positively (demonstrates potential), which increases the asserted confidence and specificity relative to the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We identified 4.3 million common protein-altering variants with orthologs in humans from whole genome sequencing data of 809 individuals across 233 primate species. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We identified 4.3 million common protein-altering variants with orthologs in humans from whole-genome sequencing data of 809 individuals from 233 primate species. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The identification of 4.3 million variants is stated equivalently in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: These variants can be inferred to have non-deleterious effects in humans based on their presence at high allele frequencies in other primate populations. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: These variants can be inferred to have nondeleterious effects in humans based on their presence at high allele frequencies in other primate populations. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about inferring nondeleterious effects is preserved without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We classified 6% of all possible human protein-altering variants as likely benign and imputed the pathogenicity of the remaining 94% using deep learning. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We classify 6% of all possible human protein-altering variants as likely benign and impute the pathogenicity of the remaining 94% of variants using deep learning. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The classification and imputation using deep learning are reported with the same figures and interpretation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning classifier achieved state-of-the-art accuracy for diagnosing pathogenic variants in patients with genetic diseases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our approach achieves state-of-the-art accuracy for diagnosing pathogenic variants in patients with genetic diseases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication asserts the same performance claim for the classifier/approach as in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The BrainAlignNet can align pairs of images of the bending C. elegans head with single pixel-level accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The BrainAlignNet network aligns pairs of images of the bending C. elegans head with single-pixel-level accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Wording and claim are effectively identical, reporting the same single-pixel alignment accuracy for BrainAlignNet.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: When incorporated into an image analysis pipeline, BrainAlignNet can link neurons over time with 99.6% accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: When incorporated into an image analysis pipeline, BrainAlignNet can link neurons over time with 99.6% accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same quantitative accuracy claim without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AutoCellLabeler labels over 100 different cell types per animal with 98% accuracy, exceeding individual human labeler performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
